content.text: Tell me how to deploy this in my homelab. Can I share my terminal with you?

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll take a look at what's in this project first so I can give you accurate deployment steps.

1	# homelab-iac
2	
3	Ansible-driven, git-versioned deployment of the netns-VPN-jail homelab
4	(thurgadin / convoker / svc-download / svc-media). This is the automation
5	layer over the `homelab-vpn-bundle` design — same architecture, same gates,
6	now runnable as one command with a check-back-later ntfy ping.
7	
8	Run everything from the MBP. It talks to the PVE **API** (token) for VM
9	lifecycle and **SSH** for in-guest config. cloud-init stays minimal (identity
10	+ packages); Ansible owns everything after first boot, so day-two changes and
11	drift checks use the same code.
12	
13	## What is and isn't automated
14	
15	| Phase (DEPLOY.md)            | Here?  | Why |
16	|------------------------------|--------|-----|
17	| 1 TrueNAS storage            | **No** | One-time; the encryption reboot test you *want* to witness. Do it by hand. |
18	| 2 PVE host prep (RAM, order) | Partly | Snippet content + boot order are one-liners in DEPLOY; do those once. |
19	| 3/5 VM creation              | Yes    | `pve_vm` role via the API |
20	| 4 Jail + stack               | Yes    | `svc_download` role, gates as assertions |
21	| 6 Media stack                | Yes    | `svc_media` role, incl. Tailscale + Caddy **when `vault_tailscale_authkey` is set** (skipped with a visible note otherwise) |
22	| 7 App wiring                 | No     | Clicking through SAB/Prowlarr/arr/Jellyseerr UIs |
23	| 9 Backups                    | Yes    | Nightly timers on both VMs: RomM DB dump + appdata tars (media, 03:05), arr/SAB appdata tars (download, 03:10), 14-day retention. ABS uses its built-in backup to the NFS-mounted `/config/backups` — schedule it once in the UI. **Restore is manual; test it quarterly.** |
24	| 9 ntfy server                | No     | Stand it up on svc-media once; until then every push prints a loud WARNING in play output (or point `ntfy_url` at ntfy.sh/<random-topic> to bootstrap) |
25	
26	Mullvad key generation is manual by nature.
27	
28	## One-time setup
29	
30	```bash
31	# 0. control node deps (MBP)
32	pip install "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests
33	ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml
34	
35	# 1. PVE API token (on thurgadin, once) — SCOPED in v5: pool + two storages,
36	#    privilege separation stays ON. Full command block with rationale lives in
37	#    inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example; short version:
38	pveum pool add homelab-svc --comment "svc-download + svc-media"
39	pveum user add automation@pve
40	pveum user token add automation@pve ansible                # copy the secret
41	for who in "-user automation@pve" "-token automation@pve!ansible"; do
42	  pveum aclmod /pool/homelab-svc     $who -role PVEVMAdmin
43	  pveum aclmod /storage/nvme0pool    $who -role PVEDatastoreUser
44	  pveum aclmod /storage/local        $who -role PVEDatastoreUser
45	done
46	
47	# 2. secrets
48	cp inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml
49	$EDITOR inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml      # token secret + Mullvad fields
50	ansible-vault encrypt inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml
51	
52	# 3. your public SSH key
53	$EDITOR inventory/group_vars/all.yml            # set admin_ssh_pubkey
54	
55	# 4. confirm you can reach PVE root over SSH (image fetch/snippet render use it)
56	ssh root@192.168.1.10 true
57	```
58	
59	## Deploy
60	
61	```bash
62	ansible-playbook site.yml --ask-vault-pass
63	```
64	
65	Or via the Makefile (see `make help` for all targets):
66	
67	```bash
68	make deps          # one-time: collections + python libs
69	make preflight     # syntax + inventory + ping, before a real run
70	make deploy        # full unattended deploy + ntfy
71	make check         # dry-run with diff (drift detection)
72	make verify        # gate assertions only + ntfy
73	make dl            # just svc-download   (ARGS="--tags jail" etc.)
74	make media         # just svc-media
75	make access        # (re)run tailscale + caddy on svc-media, e.g. after adding the authkey
76	make reconcile     # push cores/memory/onboot/startup onto EXISTING VMs (opt-in drift repair)
77	make vault-edit    # edit the encrypted secrets
78	```
79	
80	Every target prompts for the vault password once. To run non-interactively
81	(required for the nightly timer): `echo 'pass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600
82	.vault_pass`, then add `USE_VAULT_FILE=1` to any target. `.vault_pass` is
83	gitignored. Pass extra ansible flags with `ARGS=`, e.g.
84	`make deploy ARGS="--limit svc-download --tags jail,verify"`.
85	
86	Provisions both VMs, waits for cloud-init, builds the jail (gates block real
87	traffic until leak tests pass), starts the media stack, and pushes a green or
88	red ntfy at the end. Walk away; the phone tells you.
89	
90	## Nightly health check (systemd timer)
91	
92	`contrib/` ships a timer that runs the gate assertions nightly and only pings
93	you on failure — the green "all OK" ntfy is suppressed so a passing night is
94	silent. It also skips the fail-closed KILL test (`--skip-tags killtest`) so it
95	never interrupts in-flight downloads; run that one manually with
96	`make verify` when you want the full check.
97	
98	Install on an always-on box that has the repo + vault password (your MBP or a
99	small LAN host — **not** the PVE host):
100	
101	```bash
102	sudo cp -r homelab-iac /opt/homelab-iac        # or clone there
103	cd /opt/homelab-iac
104	echo 'YOUR_VAULT_PASS' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass
105	sudo cp contrib/systemd/homelab-verify.* /etc/systemd/system/
106	# edit User= and paths in the .service to match your box
107	sudo systemctl daemon-reload
108	sudo systemctl enable --now homelab-verify.timer
109	systemctl list-timers homelab-verify.timer     # confirm next run
110	```
111	
112	A failing gate fires an urgent ntfy (from the playbook's rescue block) and
113	marks the unit failed, so `systemctl --failed` catches it too. Test the wiring
114	any time with `sudo systemctl start homelab-verify.service` then
115	`journalctl -u homelab-verify.service -e`.
116	
117	## Slices & drift
118	
119	```bash
120	# resume just the jail on svc-download
121	ansible-playbook site.yml --limit svc-download --tags jail,verify --ask-vault-pass
122	
123	# what would change? (day-two drift — IN-GUEST config only; VM shape is
124	# create-once and gets reconciled explicitly with `make reconcile`)
125	ansible-playbook site.yml --check --diff --ask-vault-pass
126	
127	# gates only, e.g. from cron on the MBP
128	ansible-playbook verify.yml --tags verify --ask-vault-pass
129	
130	# gates only, skipping the disruptive fail-closed kill test
131	ansible-playbook verify.yml --tags verify --skip-tags killtest --ask-vault-pass
132	```
133	
134	Tags on the svc_download role: `files secrets nfs jail containers backup
135	verify`, plus `killtest` on the one fail-closed test that stops/starts the
136	jail. svc_media adds `media access backup`. Tag-sliced runs work in v5 (the
137	includes are `always`-tagged); failures still alert because rescue blocks are
138	`always`-tagged too.
139	
140	## Updates & image policy (v5)
141	
142	- **Containers:** the inert auto-update labels are GONE (there was no
143	  `podman-auto-update.timer`, so they advertised an update story that never
144	  ran). RomM is pinned to the release verified against this layout
145	  (`4.9.2`); everything else runs `:latest` at deploy time — after burn-in,
146	  pin what you have:  `podman inspect --format '{{ '{{' }}.ImageDigest{{ '}}' }}' jellyfin`
147	  then set `Image=docker.io/jellyfin/jellyfin@sha256:...` in the quadlet.
148	  Updates are a deliberate act: bump, restart, check.
149	- **Rocky image:** pinned to a dated build with exact-filename checksum
150	  verification (bump procedure in `group_vars/all.yml`). `.latest.` pins are
151	  refused by an assertion.
152	
153	## Leak canary operations (v5)
154	
155	A LEAK stops the download stack and writes `/var/lib/leak-canary/tripped`;
156	every 15-min run keeps alerting until you investigate and clear it:
157	
158	```bash
159	rm /var/lib/leak-canary/tripped
160	systemctl start dl-{sabnzbd,prowlarr,sonarr,radarr}
161	```
162	
163	Planned maintenance on the stack? `systemctl stop leak-canary.timer` first,
164	`systemctl start leak-canary.timer` after, or it will ping you about the
165	stopped services (that's it doing its job).
166	
167	## Gotchas carried over from the bundle
168	
169	- **host-backstop.nft input chain is default-drop.** It is now TEMPLATED
170	  from `lan_cidr` (`roles/svc_download/templates/host-backstop.nft.j2`), and
171	  the role asserts the rendered file matches `lan_cidr` before enabling
172	  nftables, so Ansible's own SSH survives. Tightening = change `lan_cidr`
173	  in group_vars (kept at the full /24 deliberately). Recovery is still
174	  `qm terminal 131` → `nft flush ruleset`.
175	- **peer.conf is wg(8) syntax** — the template emits only Interface/Peer;
176	  Address is WG_ADDR in vpn-netns.env, DNS in /etc/netns/vpn/resolv.conf.
177	- **RomM needs TWO env files** before it starts — `romm.env` (app secrets)
178	  AND `romm-db.env` (MariaDB bootstrap only; v5 splits them so provider API
179	  creds never enter the DB container). Required keys are in the quadlet
180	  headers; the role fails loudly listing them if either file is missing.
181	- **First run, no ntfy yet?** Pushes fail with a visible WARNING (not
182	  silently as before). Point `ntfy_url` at `https://ntfy.sh/<long-random>`
183	  in group_vars if you want pings before Phase 9.
184	- **PVE self-signed cert**: if you haven't put a trusted cert on :8006, set
185	  `pve_validate_certs: false` (default in group_vars/pve.yml).
186	
187	## What this doesn't give you that Terraform would
188	
189	Declarative VM *state* with a plan/apply/destroy lifecycle and a state file.
190	For two VMs on one node that's not worth the ceremony — `proxmox_kvm` is
191	idempotent enough (won't recreate an existing VMID), and `--check` covers
192	drift on the config side. If you later want true declarative infra, the
193	`bpg/proxmox` provider is the maintained choice; this repo's roles would
194	still do the in-guest half.
195

file.content:
# homelab-iac

Ansible-driven, git-versioned deployment of the netns-VPN-jail homelab
(thurgadin / convoker / svc-download / svc-media). This is the automation
layer over the `homelab-vpn-bundle` design — same architecture, same gates,
now runnable as one command with a check-back-later ntfy ping.

Run everything from the MBP. It talks to the PVE **API** (token) for VM
lifecycle and **SSH** for in-guest config. cloud-init stays minimal (identity
+ packages); Ansible owns everything after first boot, so day-two changes and
drift checks use the same code.

## What is and isn't automated

| Phase (DEPLOY.md)            | Here?  | Why |
|------------------------------|--------|-----|
| 1 TrueNAS storage            | **No** | One-time; the encryption reboot test you *want* to witness. Do it by hand. |
| 2 PVE host prep (RAM, order) | Partly | Snippet content + boot order are one-liners in DEPLOY; do those once. |
| 3/5 VM creation              | Yes    | `pve_vm` role via the API |
| 4 Jail + stack               | Yes    | `svc_download` role, gates as assertions |
| 6 Media stack                | Yes    | `svc_media` role, incl. Tailscale + Caddy **when `vault_tailscale_authkey` is set** (skipped with a visible note otherwise) |
| 7 App wiring                 | No     | Clicking through SAB/Prowlarr/arr/Jellyseerr UIs |
| 9 Backups                    | Yes    | Nightly timers on both VMs: RomM DB dump + appdata tars (media, 03:05), arr/SAB appdata tars (download, 03:10), 14-day retention. ABS uses its built-in backup to the NFS-mounted `/config/backups` — schedule it once in the UI. **Restore is manual; test it quarterly.** |
| 9 ntfy server                | No     | Stand it up on svc-media once; until then every push prints a loud WARNING in play output (or point `ntfy_url` at ntfy.sh/<random-topic> to bootstrap) |

Mullvad key generation is manual by nature.

## One-time setup

```bash
# 0. control node deps (MBP)
pip install "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests
ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml

# 1. PVE API token (on thurgadin, once) — SCOPED in v5: pool + two storages,
#    privilege separation stays ON. Full command block with rationale lives in
#    inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example; short version:
pveum pool add homelab-svc --comment "svc-download + svc-media"
pveum user add automation@pve
pveum user token add automation@pve ansible                # copy the secret
for who in "-user automation@pve" "-token automation@pve!ansible"; do
  pveum aclmod /pool/homelab-svc     $who -role PVEVMAdmin
  pveum aclmod /storage/nvme0pool    $who -role PVEDatastoreUser
  pveum aclmod /storage/local        $who -role PVEDatastoreUser
done

# 2. secrets
cp inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml
$EDITOR inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml      # token secret + Mullvad fields
ansible-vault encrypt inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml

# 3. your public SSH key
$EDITOR inventory/group_vars/all.yml            # set admin_ssh_pubkey

# 4. confirm you can reach PVE root over SSH (image fetch/snippet render use it)
ssh root@192.168.1.10 true
```

## Deploy

```bash
ansible-playbook site.yml --ask-vault-pass
```

Or via the Makefile (see `make help` for all targets):

```bash
make deps          # one-time: collections + python libs
make preflight     # syntax + inventory + ping, before a real run
make deploy        # full unattended deploy + ntfy
make check         # dry-run with diff (drift detection)
make verify        # gate assertions only + ntfy
make dl            # just svc-download   (ARGS="--tags jail" etc.)
make media         # just svc-media
make access        # (re)run tailscale + caddy on svc-media, e.g. after adding the authkey
make reconcile     # push cores/memory/onboot/startup onto EXISTING VMs (opt-in drift repair)
make vault-edit    # edit the encrypted secrets
```

Every target prompts for the vault password once. To run non-interactively
(required for the nightly timer): `echo 'pass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600
.vault_pass`, then add `USE_VAULT_FILE=1` to any target. `.vault_pass` is
gitignored. Pass extra ansible flags with `ARGS=`, e.g.
`make deploy ARGS="--limit svc-download --tags jail,verify"`.

Provisions both VMs, waits for cloud-init, builds the jail (gates block real
traffic until leak tests pass), starts the media stack, and pushes a green or
red ntfy at the end. Walk away; the phone tells you.

## Nightly health check (systemd timer)

`contrib/` ships a timer that runs the gate assertions nightly and only pings
you on failure — the green "all OK" ntfy is suppressed so a passing night is
silent. It also skips the fail-closed KILL test (`--skip-tags killtest`) so it
never interrupts in-flight downloads; run that one manually with
`make verify` when you want the full check.

Install on an always-on box that has the repo + vault password (your MBP or a
small LAN host — **not** the PVE host):

```bash
sudo cp -r homelab-iac /opt/homelab-iac        # or clone there
cd /opt/homelab-iac
echo 'YOUR_VAULT_PASS' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass
sudo cp contrib/systemd/homelab-verify.* /etc/systemd/system/
# edit User= and paths in the .service to match your box
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now homelab-verify.timer
systemctl list-timers homelab-verify.timer     # confirm next run
```

A failing gate fires an urgent ntfy (from the playbook's rescue block) and
marks the unit failed, so `systemctl --failed` catches it too. Test the wiring
any time with `sudo systemctl start homelab-verify.service` then
`journalctl -u homelab-verify.service -e`.

## Slices & drift

```bash
# resume just the jail on svc-download
ansible-playbook site.yml --limit svc-download --tags jail,verify --ask-vault-pass

# what would change? (day-two drift — IN-GUEST config only; VM shape is
# create-once and gets reconciled explicitly with `make reconcile`)
ansible-playbook site.yml --check --diff --ask-vault-pass

# gates only, e.g. from cron on the MBP
ansible-playbook verify.yml --tags verify --ask-vault-pass

# gates only, skipping the disruptive fail-closed kill test
ansible-playbook verify.yml --tags verify --skip-tags killtest --ask-vault-pass
```

Tags on the svc_download role: `files secrets nfs jail containers backup
verify`, plus `killtest` on the one fail-closed test that stops/starts the
jail. svc_media adds `media access backup`. Tag-sliced runs work in v5 (the
includes are `always`-tagged); failures still alert because rescue blocks are
`always`-tagged too.

## Updates & image policy (v5)

- **Containers:** the inert auto-update labels are GONE (there was no
  `podman-auto-update.timer`, so they advertised an update story that never
  ran). RomM is pinned to the release verified against this layout
  (`4.9.2`); everything else runs `:latest` at deploy time — after burn-in,
  pin what you have:  `podman inspect --format '{{ '{{' }}.ImageDigest{{ '}}' }}' jellyfin`
  then set `Image=docker.io/jellyfin/jellyfin@sha256:...` in the quadlet.
  Updates are a deliberate act: bump, restart, check.
- **Rocky image:** pinned to a dated build with exact-filename checksum
  verification (bump procedure in `group_vars/all.yml`). `.latest.` pins are
  refused by an assertion.

## Leak canary operations (v5)

A LEAK stops the download stack and writes `/var/lib/leak-canary/tripped`;
every 15-min run keeps alerting until you investigate and clear it:

```bash
rm /var/lib/leak-canary/tripped
systemctl start dl-{sabnzbd,prowlarr,sonarr,radarr}
```

Planned maintenance on the stack? `systemctl stop leak-canary.timer` first,
`systemctl start leak-canary.timer` after, or it will ping you about the
stopped services (that's it doing its job).

## Gotchas carried over from the bundle

- **host-backstop.nft input chain is default-drop.** It is now TEMPLATED
  from `lan_cidr` (`roles/svc_download/templates/host-backstop.nft.j2`), and
  the role asserts the rendered file matches `lan_cidr` before enabling
  nftables, so Ansible's own SSH survives. Tightening = change `lan_cidr`
  in group_vars (kept at the full /24 deliberately). Recovery is still
  `qm terminal 131` → `nft flush ruleset`.
- **peer.conf is wg(8) syntax** — the template emits only Interface/Peer;
  Address is WG_ADDR in vpn-netns.env, DNS in /etc/netns/vpn/resolv.conf.
- **RomM needs TWO env files** before it starts — `romm.env` (app secrets)
  AND `romm-db.env` (MariaDB bootstrap only; v5 splits them so provider API
  creds never enter the DB container). Required keys are in the quadlet
  headers; the role fails loudly listing them if either file is missing.
- **First run, no ntfy yet?** Pushes fail with a visible WARNING (not
  silently as before). Point `ntfy_url` at `https://ntfy.sh/<long-random>`
  in group_vars if you want pings before Phase 9.
- **PVE self-signed cert**: if you haven't put a trusted cert on :8006, set
  `pve_validate_certs: false` (default in group_vars/pve.yml).

## What this doesn't give you that Terraform would

Declarative VM *state* with a plan/apply/destroy lifecycle and a state file.
For two VMs on one node that's not worth the ceremony — `proxmox_kvm` is
idempotent enough (won't recreate an existing VMID), and `--check` covers
drift on the config side. If you later want true declarative infra, the
`bpg/proxmox` provider is the maintained choice; this repo's roles would
still do the in-guest half.

content.content:
1	# homelab-iac — task runner.
2	#
3	# Vault password handling (pick one):
4	#   1. Interactive:   every target prompts once via --ask-vault-pass (default).
5	#   2. Password file: `echo 'yourpass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass`
6	#      then run any target with USE_VAULT_FILE=1, e.g. `make deploy USE_VAULT_FILE=1`.
7	#      .vault_pass is .gitignored. This is what the systemd timer uses.
8	#
9	# Extra ansible args pass through via ARGS, e.g.:
10	#   make deploy ARGS="--limit svc-download --tags jail,verify"
11	#   make check  ARGS="--limit svc-media"
12	
13	SHELL       := /bin/bash
14	PLAYBOOK    := site.yml
15	VERIFY      := verify.yml
16	ANSIBLE     := ansible-playbook
17	LINT        := ansible-lint
18	
19	ifeq ($(USE_VAULT_FILE),1)
20	  VAULT := --vault-password-file .vault_pass
21	else
22	  VAULT := --ask-vault-pass
23	endif
24	
25	.DEFAULT_GOAL := help
26	
27	.PHONY: help deps preflight deploy dl media check verify drift reconcile access ping lint vault-edit clean
28	
29	help: ## Show this help
30		@grep -E '^[a-zA-Z_-]+:.*?## .*$$' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) \
31		  | awk 'BEGIN{FS=":.*?## "}{printf "  \033[36m%-14s\033[0m %s\n", $$1, $$2}'
32	
33	deps: ## Install control-node deps (collections + python libs)
34		pip install "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests
35		ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml
36	
37	preflight: ## Cheap checks before a real run: syntax + inventory + connectivity
38		$(ANSIBLE) --syntax-check $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY)
39		ansible-inventory --list >/dev/null && echo "inventory OK"
40		@echo "Ping service VMs (only works once they exist):"
41		-ansible service_vms -m ping
42	
43	deploy: ## Full unattended deploy (provision + configure + verify + ntfy)
44		$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) $(ARGS)
45	
46	dl: ## Configure/verify only svc-download
47		$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-download $(ARGS)
48	
49	media: ## Configure/verify only svc-media
50		$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-media $(ARGS)
51	
52	check: ## Dry-run the full deploy (no changes) with a diff
53		$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --check --diff $(ARGS)
54	
55	drift: check ## Alias for check — see what has drifted since last apply
56	
57	reconcile: ## Push cores/memory/onboot/startup from host_vars onto EXISTING VMs (opt-in)

file.content:
# homelab-iac — task runner.
#
# Vault password handling (pick one):
#   1. Interactive:   every target prompts once via --ask-vault-pass (default).
#   2. Password file: `echo 'yourpass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass`
#      then run any target with USE_VAULT_FILE=1, e.g. `make deploy USE_VAULT_FILE=1`.
#      .vault_pass is .gitignored. This is what the systemd timer uses.
#
# Extra ansible args pass through via ARGS, e.g.:
#   make deploy ARGS="--limit svc-download --tags jail,verify"
#   make check  ARGS="--limit svc-media"

SHELL       := /bin/bash
PLAYBOOK    := site.yml
VERIFY      := verify.yml
ANSIBLE     := ansible-playbook
LINT        := ansible-lint

ifeq ($(USE_VAULT_FILE),1)
  VAULT := --vault-password-file .vault_pass
else
  VAULT := --ask-vault-pass
endif

.DEFAULT_GOAL := help

.PHONY: help deps preflight deploy dl media check verify drift reconcile access ping lint vault-edit clean

help: ## Show this help
	@grep -E '^[a-zA-Z_-]+:.*?## .*$$' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) \
	  | awk 'BEGIN{FS=":.*?## "}{printf "  \033[36m%-14s\033[0m %s\n", $$1, $$2}'

deps: ## Install control-node deps (collections + python libs)
	pip install "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests
	ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml

preflight: ## Cheap checks before a real run: syntax + inventory + connectivity
	$(ANSIBLE) --syntax-check $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY)
	ansible-inventory --list >/dev/null && echo "inventory OK"
	@echo "Ping service VMs (only works once they exist):"
	-ansible service_vms -m ping

deploy: ## Full unattended deploy (provision + configure + verify + ntfy)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) $(ARGS)

dl: ## Configure/verify only svc-download
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-download $(ARGS)

media: ## Configure/verify only svc-media
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-media $(ARGS)

check: ## Dry-run the full deploy (no changes) with a diff
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --check --diff $(ARGS)

drift: check ## Alias for check — see what has drifted since last apply

reconcile: ## Push cores/memory/onboot/startup from host_vars onto EXISTING VMs (opt-in)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --tags provision -e pve_reconcile=true $(ARGS)

access: ## (Re)run just the svc-media access layer (tailscale + caddy)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-media --tags access $(ARGS)

verify: ## Run ONLY the gate assertions (health check) + ntfy
	$(ANSIBLE) $(VERIFY) $(VAULT) --tags verify $(ARGS)

ping: ## Ansible ping the two service VMs
	ansible service_vms -m ping $(ARGS)

lint: ## ansible-lint (install: pip install ansible-lint)
	$(LINT) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY) || echo "(ansible-lint not installed or found issues)"

vault-edit: ## Edit the encrypted secrets file
	ansible-vault edit inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml

clean: ## Remove local ansible cruft
	rm -rf *.retry .ansible collections

content.text:
Good news on the second question first: **you're already sharing your terminal with me.** Running in Claude Code, I can execute commands directly on this Mac (you approve them via permission prompts) — and that's exactly where this repo is designed to run from ("Run everything from the MBP"). The one thing I can't do is type into interactive password prompts, so for steps like `ansible-vault encrypt` you'd either enter the password when prompted in your terminal, or we use the `.vault_pass` file approach the Makefile supports. Just tell me to start and I'll drive it.

Here's the deployment path, tailored to what I found on your machine right now:

## Current state
- **Ansible isn't installed** on this Mac (only python3 found)
- **Secrets file doesn't exist yet** — only [all_vault.yml.example](inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example)
- **Your SSH pubkey is still a placeholder** (`REPLACE_ME`) in [all.yml:58](inventory/group_vars/all.yml#L58)

So you're at step zero of the one-time setup. The order is:

## 1. Control node deps (this Mac — I can do all of this)
```bash
pip install ansible "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests   # or: brew install ansible
ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml
```

## 2. Manual prerequisites (per the README, done by hand once)
- **TrueNAS storage** (Phase 1) — deliberately manual, including the encryption reboot test
- **PVE host prep** — the RAM/boot-order one-liners from DEPLOY.md
- **PVE API token** — run the `pveum` block from the README on thurgadin (creates a scoped `automation@pve!ansible` token on the `homelab-svc` pool); copy the secret
- **Mullvad WireGuard key** — generate manually

## 3. Secrets and identity (I can do this with you)
```bash
cp inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml
# fill in: PVE token secret + Mullvad fields
ansible-vault encrypt inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml
```
Plus set your real `admin_ssh_pubkey` in [all.yml](inventory/group_vars/all.yml) — I can pull it from `~/.ssh/*.pub` for you. Then confirm `ssh root@192.168.1.10 true` works (image fetch uses PVE root SSH).

## 4. Deploy
```bash
make preflight   # syntax + inventory + connectivity check
make deploy      # full run: provisions both VMs, builds the VPN jail, media stack, ntfy ping at the end
```
The playbook gates real traffic behind leak tests, so it fails safe. After it goes green: app wiring (SAB/Prowlarr/arr/Jellyseerr UIs) is manual, and eventually you install the nightly verify timer from `contrib/`.

**Want me to start?** I can do step 1 (install Ansible + deps) and prep step 3 right now. Also tell me: is the PVE API token already created on thurgadin, or is that still to do? And do you want the vault password interactive or via a `.vault_pass` file (needed later for the nightly timer anyway)?
how to share a terminal session with you? so you can view it

There's no built-in "screen share" — I can't see your VSCode terminal panes directly. But there are two easy ways to give me eyes on a session you're driving, both of which work great for things like the `pveum` steps on thurgadin:

**Option 1: tmux (best for live viewing — recommended)**

You work inside a tmux session; I peek at it whenever needed with `capture-pane`:

```bash
brew install tmux        # if you don't have it
tmux new -s homelab      # do your work in here (ssh to thurgadin, etc.)
```

Then at any point I run:
```bash
tmux capture-pane -p -t homelab -S -200
```
…and I see the last 200 lines of exactly what's on your screen — scrollback, output, errors, everything. I can also send keystrokes into it (`tmux send-keys`) if you want me to type commands into your session, so it becomes truly collaborative: you handle the password prompts, I handle the commands.

**Option 2: `script` logging (simplest, view-only)**

```bash
script -t 0 ~/homelab-session.log
# ...do your work; type `exit` when done
```
That records everything to a file (`-t 0` flushes continuously), and I just read the log as you go. Downside: terminal control characters make the log a bit noisy, and I can't interact.

**Option 3: just paste** — for one-off outputs, copy/paste into the chat works fine.

My suggestion for your deploy: start `tmux new -s homelab`, SSH to thurgadin in it, and tell me when you're in — I'll watch the pane and walk you through the PVE token setup (or type the commands in for you) while you supervise. Meanwhile everything local (installing Ansible, editing the vault, running `make deploy`) I can run directly myself as before.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
# ============================================================================
# Environment facts (audited 2026-05-31; image pin refreshed 2026-07-15).
# Non-secret — safe in git. Secrets live in group_vars/all_vault.yml
# (ansible-vault encrypted).
# ============================================================================

# --- Network / identity ---
lan_gateway:     192.168.1.1
lan_dns:         192.168.1.1
lan_cidr:        192.168.1.0/24     # admin scope (deliberate: whole flat LAN)
search_domain:   fort.wow
admin_user:      straderb
svc_uid:         10001
svc_gid:         10001
timezone:        America/New_York

# --- Hosts ---
truenas_ip:      192.168.1.20
svc_media_ip:    192.168.1.30
svc_download_ip: 192.168.1.31

# --- TrueNAS NFS (created by hand in Phase 1 — NOT automated) ---
nfs_media_export:   "/mnt/sata_wd_14tb/media"
nfs_backups_export: "/mnt/sata_wd_14tb/backups"
nfs_opts: "hard,nofail,x-systemd.automount,x-systemd.mount-timeout=30"

# --- Proxmox storage (from /etc/pve/storage.cfg) ---
pve_storage:         nvme0pool           # VM disks
pve_snippet_storage: local
pve_snippet_dir:     /var/lib/vz/snippets
pve_image_dir:       /var/lib/vz/template/cache
pve_bridge:          vmbr0
# EL10 userspace is x86-64-v3; PVE default (v2-AES) won't boot it. 'host' on
# the 3700X (Zen 2) qualifies. Use x86-64-v3 instead only for live migration.
pve_cpu_type:        host

# --- Rocky 10 GenericCloud image — PINNED (v5) --------------------------
# Pin a DATED build, never the mutable `.latest` symlink: latest can change
# upstream while the local .sha256 stamp keeps an old copy, and the old v4
# check matched the hash against ANY line of the aggregate CHECKSUM file.
# v5 fetches the per-image BSD-style CHECKSUM and matches the exact filename.
#
# BUMP PROCEDURE (deliberate, not automatic):
#   1. Browse https://dl.rockylinux.org/pub/rocky/10/images/x86_64/
#   2. Update the three values below to the new dated build.
#   3. rm the old image + .sha256 stamp in {{ pve_image_dir }} on thurgadin.
#   4. Note: Rocky prunes dated images of the PREVIOUS minor when a new one
#      lands, so a stale pin eventually 404s — that is your cue to bump.
#   Optional authenticity (not just transfer integrity): also fetch
#   {{ rocky_checksum_url }}.asc and `gpg --verify` against the Rocky key.
rocky_image_name: "Rocky-10-GenericCloud-Base-10.2-20260525.0.x86_64.qcow2"
rocky_image_url:  "https://dl.rockylinux.org/pub/rocky/10/images/x86_64/Rocky-10-GenericCloud-Base-10.2-20260525.0.x86_64.qcow2"
rocky_checksum_url: "https://dl.rockylinux.org/pub/rocky/10/images/x86_64/Rocky-10-GenericCloud-Base-10.2-20260525.0.x86_64.qcow2.CHECKSUM"

# --- Admin SSH key injected into both VMs (public key CONTENT, not a path) ---
# Paste your MBP ed25519 public key here (safe — it's a public key):
admin_ssh_pubkey: "ssh-ed25519 AAAA...REPLACE_ME... straderb@mbp"

# --- Mullvad in-tunnel DNS (public constant, not a secret) ---
mullvad_dns: "10.64.0.1"

# --- ntfy: where deploy results are pushed. ------------------------------
# Default is the LAN-internal ntfy you'll stand up on svc-media (Phase 9).
# FIRST RUN: that ntfy does not exist yet. Either point this at
# https://ntfy.sh with a long random topic for bootstrap, or accept that
# the push fails — v5 prints a loud WARNING in the play output instead of
# swallowing it (v4 was silent). The svc-download leak canary alerts to
# this same target; the host backstop opens egress to it automatically
# when the ntfy host == svc_media_ip.
ntfy_url:   "http://192.168.1.30:8080"     # LAN-internal ntfy on svc-media
ntfy_topic: "homelab-deploy"

# --- svc-media access layer -----------------------------------------------
# caddy  = install Tailscale + Caddy and wire the ts.net vhosts, fully
#          unattended IF vault_tailscale_authkey is set (skipped with a
#          visible note when tailscaled isn't authenticated yet).
# manual = v4 behavior: role stops at "containers healthy on the LAN";
#          you run `tailscale up` + Caddy by hand (DEPLOY Phase 6).
media_access_layer: caddy

lines: # --- Admin SSH key injected into both VMs (public key CONTENT, not a path) ---
# Paste your MBP ed25519 public key here (safe — it's a public key):
# --- Mullvad in-tunnel DNS (public constant, not a secret) ---

content.text:
Done on my side. Here's where we stand:

**Complete:**
- PVE: pool, user, token, and scoped ACLs — all verified ✅
- MBP: Ansible 2.21.2 + proxmoxer in `.venv`, collections installed ✅
- Your real SSH key is now set in [all.yml:58](inventory/group_vars/all.yml#L58) ✅
- [all_vault.yml](inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml) created from the example (still plaintext) ✅

**What only you can fill in** — open [inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml](inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml) in VSCode and edit these directly (keeps secrets out of our chat):
1. `vault_pve_token_secret` — the UUID that `pveum user token add` printed. If you didn't copy it, say so and I'll recreate the token via your tmux session.
2. `vault_mullvad_*` (5 fields) — from your Mullvad account: generate a WireGuard key/config at mullvad.net → the config gives you the private key, server public key, numeric endpoint IP, port, and your `10.65.x.x/32` tunnel address.
3. Optional for now: `vault_tailscale_authkey` (media access layer is skipped without it) and `vault_ntfy_token`.

**Then encrypting it** — since I can't type into interactive password prompts, easiest path (and the nightly verify timer needs it anyway): create the password file yourself in a terminal, then tell me and I'll encrypt:

```bash
echo 'YOUR_VAULT_PASSWORD' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass
```

(It's gitignored.) Once you've filled the vault fields and created `.vault_pass`, say the word — I'll encrypt the vault, run `make preflight`, and we're ready to deploy.

# homelab-iac

Ansible-driven, git-versioned deployment of the netns-VPN-jail homelab
(thurgadin / convoker / svc-download / svc-media). This is the automation
layer over the `homelab-vpn-bundle` design — same architecture, same gates,
now runnable as one command with a check-back-later ntfy ping.

Run everything from the MBP. It talks to the PVE **API** (token) for VM
lifecycle and **SSH** for in-guest config. cloud-init stays minimal (identity
+ packages); Ansible owns everything after first boot, so day-two changes and
drift checks use the same code.

## What is and isn't automated

| Phase (DEPLOY.md)            | Here?  | Why |
|------------------------------|--------|-----|
| 1 TrueNAS storage            | **No** | One-time; the encryption reboot test you *want* to witness. Do it by hand. |
| 2 PVE host prep (RAM, order) | Partly | Snippet content + boot order are one-liners in DEPLOY; do those once. |
| 3/5 VM creation              | Yes    | `pve_vm` role via the API |
| 4 Jail + stack               | Yes    | `svc_download` role, gates as assertions |
| 6 Media stack                | Yes    | `svc_media` role, incl. Tailscale + Caddy **when `vault_tailscale_authkey` is set** (skipped with a visible note otherwise) |
| 7 App wiring                 | No     | Clicking through SAB/Prowlarr/arr/Jellyseerr UIs |
| 9 Backups                    | Yes    | Nightly timers on both VMs: RomM DB dump + appdata tars (media, 03:05), arr/SAB appdata tars (download, 03:10), 14-day retention. ABS uses its built-in backup to the NFS-mounted `/config/backups` — schedule it once in the UI. **Restore is manual; test it quarterly.** |
| 9 ntfy server                | No     | Stand it up on svc-media once; until then every push prints a loud WARNING in play output (or point `ntfy_url` at ntfy.sh/<random-topic> to bootstrap) |

Mullvad key generation is manual by nature.

## One-time setup

```bash
# 0. control node deps (MBP)
pip install "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests
ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml

# 1. PVE API token (on thurgadin, once) — SCOPED in v5: pool + two storages,
#    privilege separation stays ON. Full command block with rationale lives in
#    inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example; short version:
pveum pool add homelab-svc --comment "svc-download + svc-media"
pveum user add automation@pve
pveum user token add automation@pve ansible                # copy the secret
for who in "-user automation@pve" "-token automation@pve!ansible"; do
  pveum aclmod /pool/homelab-svc     $who -role PVEVMAdmin
  pveum aclmod /storage/nvme0pool    $who -role PVEDatastoreUser
  pveum aclmod /storage/local        $who -role PVEDatastoreUser
done

# 2. secrets
cp inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml.example inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml
$EDITOR inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml      # token secret + Mullvad fields
ansible-vault encrypt inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml

# 3. your public SSH key
$EDITOR inventory/group_vars/all.yml            # set admin_ssh_pubkey

# 4. confirm you can reach PVE root over SSH (image fetch/snippet render use it)
ssh root@192.168.1.10 true
```

## Deploy

```bash
ansible-playbook site.yml --ask-vault-pass
```

Or via the Makefile (see `make help` for all targets):

```bash
make deps          # one-time: collections + python libs
make preflight     # syntax + inventory + ping, before a real run
make deploy        # full unattended deploy + ntfy
make check         # dry-run with diff (drift detection)
make verify        # gate assertions only + ntfy
make dl            # just svc-download   (ARGS="--tags jail" etc.)
make media         # just svc-media
make access        # (re)run tailscale + caddy on svc-media, e.g. after adding the authkey
make reconcile     # push cores/memory/onboot/startup onto EXISTING VMs (opt-in drift repair)
make vault-edit    # edit the encrypted secrets
```

Every target prompts for the vault password once. To run non-interactively
(required for the nightly timer): `echo 'pass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600
.vault_pass`, then add `USE_VAULT_FILE=1` to any target. `.vault_pass` is
gitignored. Pass extra ansible flags with `ARGS=`, e.g.
`make deploy ARGS="--limit svc-download --tags jail,verify"`.

Provisions both VMs, waits for cloud-init, builds the jail (gates block real
traffic until leak tests pass), starts the media stack, and pushes a green or
red ntfy at the end. Walk away; the phone tells you.

## Nightly health check (systemd timer)

`contrib/` ships a timer that runs the gate assertions nightly and only pings
you on failure — the green "all OK" ntfy is suppressed so a passing night is
silent. It also skips the fail-closed KILL test (`--skip-tags killtest`) so it
never interrupts in-flight downloads; run that one manually with
`make verify` when you want the full check.

Install on an always-on box that has the repo + vault password (your MBP or a
small LAN host — **not** the PVE host):

```bash
sudo cp -r homelab-iac /opt/homelab-iac        # or clone there
cd /opt/homelab-iac
echo 'YOUR_VAULT_PASS' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass
sudo cp contrib/systemd/homelab-verify.* /etc/systemd/system/
# edit User= and paths in the .service to match your box
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now homelab-verify.timer
systemctl list-timers homelab-verify.timer     # confirm next run
```

A failing gate fires an urgent ntfy (from the playbook's rescue block) and
marks the unit failed, so `systemctl --failed` catches it too. Test the wiring
any time with `sudo systemctl start homelab-verify.service` then
`journalctl -u homelab-verify.service -e`.

## Slices & drift

```bash
# resume just the jail on svc-download
ansible-playbook site.yml --limit svc-download --tags jail,verify --ask-vault-pass

# what would change? (day-two drift — IN-GUEST config only; VM shape is
# create-once and gets reconciled explicitly with `make reconcile`)
ansible-playbook site.yml --check --diff --ask-vault-pass

# gates only, e.g. from cron on the MBP
ansible-playbook verify.yml --tags verify --ask-vault-pass

# gates only, skipping the disruptive fail-closed kill test
ansible-playbook verify.yml --tags verify --skip-tags killtest --ask-vault-pass
```

Tags on the svc_download role: `files secrets nfs jail containers backup
verify`, plus `killtest` on the one fail-closed test that stops/starts the
jail. svc_media adds `media access backup`. Tag-sliced runs work in v5 (the
includes are `always`-tagged); failures still alert because rescue blocks are
`always`-tagged too.

## Updates & image policy (v5)

- **Containers:** the inert auto-update labels are GONE (there was no
  `podman-auto-update.timer`, so they advertised an update story that never
  ran). RomM is pinned to the release verified against this layout
  (`4.9.2`); everything else runs `:latest` at deploy time — after burn-in,
  pin what you have:  `podman inspect --format '{{ '{{' }}.ImageDigest{{ '}}' }}' jellyfin`
  then set `Image=docker.io/jellyfin/jellyfin@sha256:...` in the quadlet.
  Updates are a deliberate act: bump, restart, check.
- **Rocky image:** pinned to a dated build with exact-filename checksum
  verification (bump procedure in `group_vars/all.yml`). `.latest.` pins are
  refused by an assertion.

## Leak canary operations (v5)

A LEAK stops the download stack and writes `/var/lib/leak-canary/tripped`;
every 15-min run keeps alerting until you investigate and clear it:

```bash
rm /var/lib/leak-canary/tripped
systemctl start dl-{sabnzbd,prowlarr,sonarr,radarr}
```

Planned maintenance on the stack? `systemctl stop leak-canary.timer` first,
`systemctl start leak-canary.timer` after, or it will ping you about the
stopped services (that's it doing its job).

## Gotchas carried over from the bundle

- **host-backstop.nft input chain is default-drop.** It is now TEMPLATED
  from `lan_cidr` (`roles/svc_download/templates/host-backstop.nft.j2`), and
  the role asserts the rendered file matches `lan_cidr` before enabling
  nftables, so Ansible's own SSH survives. Tightening = change `lan_cidr`
  in group_vars (kept at the full /24 deliberately). Recovery is still
  `qm terminal 131` → `nft flush ruleset`.
- **peer.conf is wg(8) syntax** — the template emits only Interface/Peer;
  Address is WG_ADDR in vpn-netns.env, DNS in /etc/netns/vpn/resolv.conf.
- **RomM needs TWO env files** before it starts — `romm.env` (app secrets)
  AND `romm-db.env` (MariaDB bootstrap only; v5 splits them so provider API
  creds never enter the DB container). Required keys are in the quadlet
  headers; the role fails loudly listing them if either file is missing.
- **First run, no ntfy yet?** Pushes fail with a visible WARNING (not
  silently as before). Point `ntfy_url` at `https://ntfy.sh/<long-random>`
  in group_vars if you want pings before Phase 9.
- **PVE self-signed cert**: if you haven't put a trusted cert on :8006, set
  `pve_validate_certs: false` (default in group_vars/pve.yml).

## What this doesn't give you that Terraform would

Declarative VM *state* with a plan/apply/destroy lifecycle and a state file.
For two VMs on one node that's not worth the ceremony — `proxmox_kvm` is
idempotent enough (won't recreate an existing VMID), and `--check` covers
drift on the config side. If you later want true declarative infra, the
`bpg/proxmox` provider is the maintained choice; this repo's roles would
still do the in-guest half.

VERIFY      := verify.yml
# Prefer the project venv (created by `make deps`) when it exists.
BIN         := $(if $(wildcard .venv/bin/ansible-playbook),.venv/bin/,)
ANSIBLE     := $(BIN)ansible-playbook
LINT        := $(BIN)ansible-lint

input.new_string:
$(ANSIBLE) --syntax-check $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY)
	$(BIN)ansible-inventory --list >/dev/null && echo "inventory OK"
	@echo "Ping service VMs (only works once they exist):"

ping: ## Ansible ping the two service VMs
	ansible service_vms -m ping $(ARGS)
input.new_string:
ping: ## Ansible ping the two service VMs
	$(BIN)ansible service_vms -m ping $(ARGS)

	ansible-vault edit inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml
input.new_string:
vault-edit: ## Edit the encrypted secrets file
	$(BIN)ansible-vault edit inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml

# Prefer the project venv (created by `make deps`) when it exists.
BIN         := $(if $(wildcard .venv/bin/ansible-playbook),.venv/bin/,)
ANSIBLE     := $(BIN)ansible-playbook
LINT        := $(BIN)ansible-lint
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# homelab-iac — task runner.
#
# Vault password handling (pick one):
#   1. Interactive:   every target prompts once via --ask-vault-pass (default).
#   2. Password file: `echo 'yourpass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass`
#      then run any target with USE_VAULT_FILE=1, e.g. `make deploy USE_VAULT_FILE=1`.
#      .vault_pass is .gitignored. This is what the systemd timer uses.
#
# Extra ansible args pass through via ARGS, e.g.:
#   make deploy ARGS="--limit svc-download --tags jail,verify"
#   make check  ARGS="--limit svc-media"

SHELL       := /bin/bash
PLAYBOOK    := site.yml
VERIFY      := verify.yml
ANSIBLE     := ansible-playbook
LINT        := ansible-lint

ifeq ($(USE_VAULT_FILE),1)
  VAULT := --vault-password-file .vault_pass
else
  VAULT := --ask-vault-pass
endif

.DEFAULT_GOAL := help

.PHONY: help deps preflight deploy dl media check verify drift reconcile access ping lint vault-edit clean

help: ## Show this help
	@grep -E '^[a-zA-Z_-]+:.*?## .*$$' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) \
	  | awk 'BEGIN{FS=":.*?## "}{printf "  \033[36m%-14s\033[0m %s\n", $$1, $$2}'

deps: ## Install control-node deps (collections + python libs)
	pip install "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests
	ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml

preflight: ## Cheap checks before a real run: syntax + inventory + connectivity
	$(ANSIBLE) --syntax-check $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY)
	ansible-inventory --list >/dev/null && echo "inventory OK"
	@echo "Ping service VMs (only works once they exist):"
	-ansible service_vms -m ping

deploy: ## Full unattended deploy (provision + configure + verify + ntfy)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) $(ARGS)

dl: ## Configure/verify only svc-download
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-download $(ARGS)

media: ## Configure/verify only svc-media
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-media $(ARGS)

check: ## Dry-run the full deploy (no changes) with a diff
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --check --diff $(ARGS)

drift: check ## Alias for check — see what has drifted since last apply

reconcile: ## Push cores/memory/onboot/startup from host_vars onto EXISTING VMs (opt-in)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --tags provision -e pve_reconcile=true $(ARGS)

access: ## (Re)run just the svc-media access layer (tailscale + caddy)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-media --tags access $(ARGS)

verify: ## Run ONLY the gate assertions (health check) + ntfy
	$(ANSIBLE) $(VERIFY) $(VAULT) --tags verify $(ARGS)

ping: ## Ansible ping the two service VMs
	ansible service_vms -m ping $(ARGS)

lint: ## ansible-lint (install: pip install ansible-lint)
	$(LINT) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY) || echo "(ansible-lint not installed or found issues)"

vault-edit: ## Edit the encrypted secrets file
	ansible-vault edit inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml

clean: ## Remove local ansible cruft
	rm -rf *.retry .ansible collections

input.command:
git add -A && git status --porcelain | wc -l && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Initial commit: homelab-iac v5 with control-node setup fixes

Doc/tooling updates from first real deployment run:
- README: control-node deps now use a project venv (Homebrew python
  blocks global pip installs via PEP 668)
- README: set +H must precede the pveum aclmod loop on its own line —
  bash history expansion breaks on the !ansible token id
- Makefile: auto-detect .venv/bin for all ansible commands; make deps
  creates the venv
- gitignore: .venv/ and Claude Code local settings

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git log --oneline -1 && git show --stat HEAD | tail -5
input.description: Stage all files and create initial commit

content.text:
All clean and committed. Here's the summary:

**Commit `a92072d` on `main`** — 112 files, authored as Brandon Strader <brandon.strader@gmail.com>:
- README: venv-based control-node deps (Homebrew Python blocks global pip), and the `set +H` gotcha documented above the `pveum` ACL loop — must be its own line, since bash history expansion mangles `!ansible`
- Makefile: all targets auto-detect `.venv/bin`, and `make deps` now creates the venv
- `.gitignore`: added `.venv/` and my local Claude settings file
- Verified excluded: plaintext `all_vault.yml`, `.vault_pass`; and `provision.env` contains no secrets (just a path comment)

**One step only you can do — GitHub auth.** `gh` isn't logged in, and the login flow is interactive. In any local terminal (your VSCode terminal is fine, doesn't need to be the tmux one):

```bash
gh auth login
```

Pick GitHub.com → HTTPS → login with browser. Tell me when it's done and I'll create the repo as **private** (it's homelab infra with your internal IPs and network layout — say so if you want it public) and push `main` up. If you'd rather push to an existing GitHub repo instead of a new `homelab-iac` one, give me its name.

lastPrompt: Before continuing, update the instructions and whatever else, then commit it to github

content.text:
The repo is live and everything's committed. Picking up where we left off, the remaining steps before deploy:

1. **You**: fill in [all_vault.yml](inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml) — PVE token secret (tell me if you lost it and I'll recreate the token via tmux) + the 5 Mullvad fields
2. **You**: `echo 'YOUR_VAULT_PASSWORD' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass`
3. **Me**: encrypt the vault, `make preflight`, then `make deploy`

Say the word when 1 and 2 are done.

<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file /Users/straderb/Documents/homelab/arr-stack-v1/20260716/homelab-iac/inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>

Verify step1 is good. wasn't sure what to put in for wg_addr. 

do I pick my own "YOUR_VAULT_PASSWORD"?

Yes — the vault password is one you invent yourself: it just encrypts the secrets file locally (and lives in `.vault_pass` so the nightly timer can decrypt). Make it long and random; it never leaves your machine.

Now let me verify what you filled in:

content.content:
1	---
2	# ============================================================================
3	# SECRETS. Copy to all_vault.yml and encrypt:
4	#     cp all_vault.yml.example all_vault.yml
5	#     $EDITOR all_vault.yml          # fill in real values
6	#     ansible-vault encrypt all_vault.yml
7	# Edit later with:  ansible-vault edit all_vault.yml
8	# all_vault.yml is .gitignored; only the .example (this file) is committed.
9	# ============================================================================
10	
11	# --- Proxmox API token (Phase 0 on thurgadin) — SCOPED (v5) ---------------
12	# v4 granted PVEVMAdmin at / with privilege separation disabled. v5 scopes
13	# the token to a pool holding only these two VMs + the two storages it
14	# actually touches, and keeps privsep ON (ACLs go on the token itself).
15	# Run ON thurgadin, once:
16	#
17	#   pveum pool add homelab-svc --comment "svc-download + svc-media"
18	#   pveum user add automation@pve
19	#   pveum user token add automation@pve ansible          # privsep stays 1
20	#   # VM lifecycle inside the pool only:
21	#   pveum aclmod /pool/homelab-svc -user automation@pve -role PVEVMAdmin
22	#   pveum aclmod /pool/homelab-svc -token 'automation@pve!ansible' -role PVEVMAdmin
23	#   # Disk allocation on the VM storage + snippet/image storage:
24	#   pveum aclmod /storage/nvme0pool -user automation@pve -role PVEDatastoreUser
25	#   pveum aclmod /storage/nvme0pool -token 'automation@pve!ansible' -role PVEDatastoreUser
26	#   pveum aclmod /storage/local     -user automation@pve -role PVEDatastoreUser
27	#   pveum aclmod /storage/local     -token 'automation@pve!ansible' -role PVEDatastoreUser
28	#
29	# Honest scope note: the qm import / EFI-disk steps run as root over SSH
30	# anyway (module limitation), so this scoping protects against TOKEN leakage,
31	# not against compromise of the control node's SSH key. If the first create
32	# 403s on some edge permission, widen deliberately (add Sys.Audit on /nodes/
33	# thurgadin via a custom role) rather than reverting to PVEVMAdmin-at-/.
34	vault_pve_token_id:     "automation@pve!ansible"
35	vault_pve_token_secret: "[REDACTED-PVE-TOKEN-SECRET]"
36	
37	# --- Mullvad WireGuard (from your generated config; wg(8) fields only) ---
38	vault_mullvad_privkey:    "[REDACTED-MULLVAD-PRIVKEY]"
39	vault_mullvad_peer_pubkey: "[REDACTED-MULLVAD-PEER-PUBKEY]"
40	vault_mullvad_endpoint_ip: "[REDACTED-MULLVAD-ENDPOINT-IP]"      # NUMERIC endpoint IP
41	# Any port your generated config uses is fine now — v5 derives BOTH the
42	# nftables endpoint-IP set and the endpoint-PORT set from peer.conf, so the
43	# backstop no longer assumes 51820.
44	vault_mullvad_endpoint_port: 51820
45	vault_mullvad_wg_addr:    "10.65.x.x/32"     # your assigned tunnel Address
46	
47	# --- Tailscale (svc-media) -------------------------------------------------
48	# Pre-auth key from https://login.tailscale.com/admin/settings/keys
49	#   Recommended: Reusable=NO, Ephemeral=NO, Pre-authorized=YES,
50	#   Tags: tag:svc-media (if you use ACL tags), short expiry — the key is
51	#   only needed once; the node keeps its own credentials afterwards.
52	# Leave "" to keep `tailscale up` a manual step (role skips with a note,
53	# and Caddy is skipped too since it binds to the tailscale address).
54	vault_tailscale_authkey: ""
55	
56	# --- ntfy auth (only if your ntfy requires a token; else leave empty) ---
57	vault_ntfy_token: ""

file.content:
---
# ============================================================================
# SECRETS. Copy to all_vault.yml and encrypt:
#     cp all_vault.yml.example all_vault.yml
#     $EDITOR all_vault.yml          # fill in real values
#     ansible-vault encrypt all_vault.yml
# Edit later with:  ansible-vault edit all_vault.yml
# all_vault.yml is .gitignored; only the .example (this file) is committed.
# ============================================================================

# --- Proxmox API token (Phase 0 on thurgadin) — SCOPED (v5) ---------------
# v4 granted PVEVMAdmin at / with privilege separation disabled. v5 scopes
# the token to a pool holding only these two VMs + the two storages it
# actually touches, and keeps privsep ON (ACLs go on the token itself).
# Run ON thurgadin, once:
#
#   pveum pool add homelab-svc --comment "svc-download + svc-media"
#   pveum user add automation@pve
#   pveum user token add automation@pve ansible          # privsep stays 1
#   # VM lifecycle inside the pool only:
#   pveum aclmod /pool/homelab-svc -user automation@pve -role PVEVMAdmin
#   pveum aclmod /pool/homelab-svc -token 'automation@pve!ansible' -role PVEVMAdmin
#   # Disk allocation on the VM storage + snippet/image storage:
#   pveum aclmod /storage/nvme0pool -user automation@pve -role PVEDatastoreUser
#   pveum aclmod /storage/nvme0pool -token 'automation@pve!ansible' -role PVEDatastoreUser
#   pveum aclmod /storage/local     -user automation@pve -role PVEDatastoreUser
#   pveum aclmod /storage/local     -token 'automation@pve!ansible' -role PVEDatastoreUser
#
# Honest scope note: the qm import / EFI-disk steps run as root over SSH
# anyway (module limitation), so this scoping protects against TOKEN leakage,
# not against compromise of the control node's SSH key. If the first create
# 403s on some edge permission, widen deliberately (add Sys.Audit on /nodes/
# thurgadin via a custom role) rather than reverting to PVEVMAdmin-at-/.
vault_pve_token_id:     "automation@pve!ansible"
vault_pve_token_secret: "[REDACTED-PVE-TOKEN-SECRET]"

# --- Mullvad WireGuard (from your generated config; wg(8) fields only) ---
vault_mullvad_privkey:    "[REDACTED-MULLVAD-PRIVKEY]"
vault_mullvad_peer_pubkey: "[REDACTED-MULLVAD-PEER-PUBKEY]"
vault_mullvad_endpoint_ip: "[REDACTED-MULLVAD-ENDPOINT-IP]"      # NUMERIC endpoint IP
# Any port your generated config uses is fine now — v5 derives BOTH the
# nftables endpoint-IP set and the endpoint-PORT set from peer.conf, so the
# backstop no longer assumes 51820.
vault_mullvad_endpoint_port: 51820
vault_mullvad_wg_addr:    "10.65.x.x/32"     # your assigned tunnel Address

# --- Tailscale (svc-media) -------------------------------------------------
# Pre-auth key from https://login.tailscale.com/admin/settings/keys
#   Recommended: Reusable=NO, Ephemeral=NO, Pre-authorized=YES,
#   Tags: tag:svc-media (if you use ACL tags), short expiry — the key is
#   only needed once; the node keeps its own credentials afterwards.
# Leave "" to keep `tailscale up` a manual step (role skips with a note,
# and Caddy is skipped too since it binds to the tailscale address).
vault_tailscale_authkey: ""

# --- ntfy auth (only if your ntfy requires a token; else leave empty) ---
vault_ntfy_token: ""

lastPrompt: Verify step1 is good. wasn't sure what to put in for wg_addr.   do I pick my own "YOUR_VAULT_PASSWORD"?

content.text:
Step 1 is almost good — everything is filled in correctly except the one you flagged:

- **Token secret** ✅ — proper UUID format (so you did copy it, great)
- **Mullvad privkey / peer pubkey** ✅ — both valid WireGuard base64 keys
- **Endpoint IP** ✅ — numeric, as required
- **Port** ✅ — 51820
- **`vault_mullvad_wg_addr`** ❌ — still the `10.65.x.x/32` placeholder

**Where to find wg_addr:** it's in the same Mullvad WireGuard config that gave you the private key. Open that `.conf` file (or the config-generator page at mullvad.net/account) and look at the `[Interface]` section:

```ini
[Interface]
PrivateKey = eOQh...
Address = [REDACTED-EXAMPLE-WG-ADDR]/32, fc00:bbbb:...:/128   ← this line
```

Copy the **IPv4 one only** (the `10.x.y.z/32`) into `vault_mullvad_wg_addr`. It's the tunnel address Mullvad assigned to that specific key — the two are a pair, so it must come from the same generated config, not made up. If you no longer have the config file, regenerate one on the Mullvad site (that gives you a new privkey + address pair — then update both fields).

Two other notes:
- `vault_tailscale_authkey` is empty — that's fine; the play will skip Tailscale/Caddy on svc-media with a visible note, and we can run `make access` later once you add a key.
- And yes (repeating since you asked): the vault password is **your own invention** — pick something long, it only encrypts this file locally.

So: paste the Address into wg_addr, create `.vault_pass` with your chosen password (`echo 'yourpass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass`), then tell me and I'll encrypt + preflight.

file.content:
---
# Physical/logical hosts. The two service VMs won't answer SSH until pve_vm
# has created them — that's why site.yml provisions before configuring, and
# why plays that touch them use `gather_facts` lazily.
all:
  children:
    # Runs API calls against PVE. These plays target localhost (the control
    # node / your MBP) and talk to the PVE API over HTTPS — we do NOT need
    # SSH-as-root to thurgadin for VM creation, only the API token.
    pve:
      hosts:
        thurgadin:
          ansible_connection: local   # API-driven; see group_vars/pve.yml
          pve_api_host: 192.168.1.10
          pve_node: thurgadin

    # In-guest configuration targets. SSH as straderb, become root.
    service_vms:
      hosts:
        svc-download:
          ansible_host: 192.168.1.31
        svc-media:
          ansible_host: 192.168.1.30
      vars:
        ansible_user: straderb
        ansible_become: true
        ansible_become_method: sudo

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# homelab-iac — task runner.
#
# Vault password handling (pick one):
#   1. Interactive:   every target prompts once via --ask-vault-pass (default).
#   2. Password file: `echo 'yourpass' > .vault_pass && chmod 600 .vault_pass`
#      then run any target with USE_VAULT_FILE=1, e.g. `make deploy USE_VAULT_FILE=1`.
#      .vault_pass is .gitignored. This is what the systemd timer uses.
#
# Extra ansible args pass through via ARGS, e.g.:
#   make deploy ARGS="--limit svc-download --tags jail,verify"
#   make check  ARGS="--limit svc-media"

SHELL       := /bin/bash
PLAYBOOK    := site.yml
VERIFY      := verify.yml
# Prefer the project venv (created by `make deps`) when it exists.
BIN         := $(if $(wildcard .venv/bin/ansible-playbook),.venv/bin/,)
ANSIBLE     := $(BIN)ansible-playbook
LINT        := $(BIN)ansible-lint

ifeq ($(USE_VAULT_FILE),1)
  VAULT := --vault-password-file .vault_pass
else
  VAULT := --ask-vault-pass
endif

.DEFAULT_GOAL := help

.PHONY: help deps preflight deploy dl media check verify drift reconcile access ping lint vault-edit clean

help: ## Show this help
	@grep -E '^[a-zA-Z_-]+:.*?## .*$$' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) \
	  | awk 'BEGIN{FS=":.*?## "}{printf "  \033[36m%-14s\033[0m %s\n", $$1, $$2}'

deps: ## Install control-node deps (venv + collections + python libs)
	python3 -m venv .venv
	.venv/bin/pip install ansible "proxmoxer>=2.0" requests
	.venv/bin/ansible-galaxy collection install -r requirements.yml

preflight: ## Cheap checks before a real run: syntax + inventory + connectivity
	$(ANSIBLE) --syntax-check $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY)
	$(BIN)ansible-inventory --list >/dev/null && echo "inventory OK"
	@echo "Ping service VMs (only works once they exist):"
	-$(BIN)ansible service_vms -m ping

deploy: ## Full unattended deploy (provision + configure + verify + ntfy)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) $(ARGS)

dl: ## Configure/verify only svc-download
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-download $(ARGS)

media: ## Configure/verify only svc-media
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-media $(ARGS)

check: ## Dry-run the full deploy (no changes) with a diff
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --check --diff $(ARGS)

drift: check ## Alias for check — see what has drifted since last apply

reconcile: ## Push cores/memory/onboot/startup from host_vars onto EXISTING VMs (opt-in)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --tags provision -e pve_reconcile=true $(ARGS)

access: ## (Re)run just the svc-media access layer (tailscale + caddy)
	$(ANSIBLE) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VAULT) --limit svc-media --tags access $(ARGS)

verify: ## Run ONLY the gate assertions (health check) + ntfy
	$(ANSIBLE) $(VERIFY) $(VAULT) --tags verify $(ARGS)

ping: ## Ansible ping the two service VMs
	$(BIN)ansible service_vms -m ping $(ARGS)

lint: ## ansible-lint (install: pip install ansible-lint)
	$(LINT) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY) || echo "(ansible-lint not installed or found issues)"

vault-edit: ## Edit the encrypted secrets file
	$(BIN)ansible-vault edit inventory/group_vars/all_vault.yml

clean: ## Remove local ansible cruft
	rm -rf *.retry .ansible collections

lines: $(LINT) $(PLAYBOOK) $(VERIFY) || echo "(ansible-lint not installed or found issues)"
vault-edit: ## Edit the encrypted secrets file
